text: The surgeon performs hernia reduction on a large volume of incarcerated omentum, encountering multiple adhesive bands. Scarring is visible as the surgeon carefully lyses the final adhesions, freeing the omentum from the hernia sac.

The surgeon performs a standard transabdominal preperitoneal (TAP) repair, focusing on caudal dissection after completing the cephalad dissection. The volcano sign is visible, aiding in the reduction of the hernia. The ipsilateral side of the dissection is approximately three to four centimeters from the hernia, with a thin preperitoneal plane lacking much fat. The surgeon develops a larger flap on the contralateral side to drape over the mesh on the ipsilateral side, facilitating easier dissection and visualization.

The surgeon extends the areolar plane dissection beyond the traditional five centimeters to about ten centimeters, preparing to drape the mesh over the ipsilateral dissection. As they approach the arcuate line, they encounter an arcuate line hernia beneath the posterior sheath, with the peritoneum invaginating between the posterior sheath and the rectus muscle. This finding was not visible on the preoperative CT scan.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the semilunar line, creating a broad space to address the arcuate line hernia. The arcuate line hernia is visible, and a large area is prepared for mesh placement. The umbilical hernia is also exposed with ample space for mesh. The surgeon prepares for defect closure using a permanent V-Loc suture on a GS-21 needle, addressing a small area of diastasis with plication before sequentially tightening and closing the hernia defect.

The surgeon uses traction to sequentially tighten the suture, closing the defect gradually over two or three runs. The insufflation pressure is maintained between eight to twelve. The arcuate line hernia space is addressed by re-approximating the posterior sheath with the rectus muscle using permanent suture, aiming for good tissue apposition to eliminate dead space.

The surgeon places a large, oblong asymmetric mesh measuring approximately 22 by 28 centimeters into the preperitoneal pocket, ensuring five centimeters of circumferential coverage around the original hernia site. The mesh is positioned to extend beyond the initial peritoneal incision due to the redundancy of the peritoneum. The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to evacuate air, ensuring the mesh lays flat before completing the standard flap closure.
